bytes.topic: Testing Strategy — unit vs integration vs E2E pyramid, what to test at each level
bytes.title: Cypress Testing Pyramid — What Belongs at Each Level
bytes.body:
Cypress collapses the traditional pyramid: you write component tests AND E2E tests in the same tool, same syntax. That's powerful, but it makes the boundary between levels easy to blur.

Unit level → use `cy.mount()` (Cypress Component Testing). Mount a single component, stub props and events, assert rendered output. No routing, no network. Fast, isolated. Best for logic-heavy components: form validation, conditional rendering, custom hooks via wrapper components.

Integration level → still `cy.mount()`, but compose multiple components together. Let them communicate naturally. Use `cy.intercept()` to stub API calls at the boundary. Assert that parent-child wiring works without spinning up a full browser session.

E2E level → full `cy.visit()` flows. Reserve these for critical user journeys: sign-up, checkout, auth redirect. Don't assert UI copy here — assert state changes, URL transitions, and API calls via `cy.intercept().as()` + `cy.wait('@alias')`. E2E is expensive; keep it thin.

The practical ratio for a Cypress-only project: ~60% component tests, ~25% integration mounts, ~15% E2E flows.

bytes.topic: Test Behaviour Not Implementation — Testing Library philosophy, query by role not by class
bytes.title: Cypress — Test Behaviour Not Implementation with Accessible Queries
bytes.body:
Most Cypress tests break not because the feature broke, but because a class name changed. That's a test smell. Instead of `cy.get('.btn-primary')` or `cy.get('#submit-form')`, reach for queries that mirror how a real user interacts with the DOM — by role, label, and text.

Cypress ships with `cy.findByRole()`, `cy.findByLabelText()`, and `cy.findByText()` via the `@testing-library/cypress` plugin. These bind to semantic meaning, not implementation details. A button is still a button whether it has class `btn-v2` or `btn-v3`.

`cy.findByRole('button', { name: /submit/i })` finds the submit button by its accessible name — the same way a screen reader would. `cy.findByLabelText('Email')` targets an input through its `<label>` association, not an arbitrary `id`.

This approach also enforces accessibility by design — if your query can't find the element, neither can assistive technology. When a refactor renames CSS classes, these tests survive. When markup becomes inaccessible, they fail — exactly when they should.

bytes.topic: Component Testing — rendering, user interaction, asserting on visible output
bytes.title: Cypress Component Testing — Mount, Interact, Assert Without a Browser
bytes.body:
Cypress Component Testing lets you mount a single component in isolation using `cy.mount()`, bypassing the need for a full application or dev server route. Unlike E2E tests, the component renders directly in Cypress's browser sandbox, giving you sub-second feedback loops.

Mount your component with props, then drive it exactly as a user would — `cy.get()`, `cy.click()`, `cy.type()`. Cypress's command queue is async but reads synchronously, so no `await` is needed. Assertions use `.should()` chained off queries, and Cypress retries them automatically until the timeout.

For a button that toggles a label, mount it, click it, then assert the DOM reflects the new state. Use `cy.contains()` when asserting on visible text rather than reaching into implementation details like state variables. Stub props or callbacks with `cy.stub()` to verify emission of events without wiring up a parent.

Coverage: Cypress instruments component tests with the same Istanbul-based coverage used in E2E, so you get unified reports with no extra tooling.

bytes.topic: Unit Testing — pure functions, custom hooks with renderHook
bytes.title: Cypress Component Testing — Pure Functions and Custom Hooks Without renderHook
bytes.body:
Cypress Component Testing doesn't ship `renderHook` — that's React Testing Library's API. For pure functions, `cy.wrap()` chains directly into Cypress assertions without mounting anything. For custom hooks, the idiomatic pattern is a minimal test-harness component: a tiny React component that calls your hook, exposes its output via `data-*` attributes, and lets `cy.get()` assert on the result.

This approach gives you more than RTL's `renderHook` — you can assert on DOM side effects, fire real events with `cy.type()` or `cy.click()`, and watch state updates propagate in the Cypress runner's visual preview.

For time-based hooks like `useDebounce`, mount a harness and use `cy.clock()` with `cy.tick()` instead of Jest's fake timers. No manual setup — `cy.clock()` intercepts `setTimeout` globally the moment it's called. Keep harness components co-located with the test file, not in shared fixtures, so hook signature changes surface as immediate local breakage rather than distant fixture drift.

bytes.topic: Integration Testing — testing multiple components together, mocking at the right boundary
bytes.title: Cypress Integration Testing — Mocking at the Right Boundary
bytes.body:
Integration tests in Cypress shine when you test multiple components wired together — a form, its validation logic, and the submission handler — without mocking every internal detail. The key is mocking at the network boundary, not at the component level. Use `cy.intercept()` to stub HTTP calls and let your real components render, interact, and respond naturally.

A common mistake is over-mocking: stubbing child components or internal state, which turns your integration test into a glorified unit test. Instead, mount the parent component with `cy.mount()`, let it render its children, and only intercept the outbound API call. This validates the full render tree, event delegation, and state propagation.

Set `cy.intercept()` before `cy.mount()` so the stub is registered before any requests fire. Use `cy.wait('@alias')` to assert the request payload — confirming your components assembled the correct data before sending it. This boundary-at-the-network pattern gives you high confidence without brittle, over-mocked tests.

bytes.topic: E2E Testing — full user flows, critical paths only (auth, onboarding, core feature)
bytes.title: Cypress E2E — Testing Critical Paths Without the Noise
bytes.body:
Most Cypress suites die under maintenance load because teams test everything. E2E is expensive — reserve it for paths that break users: auth, onboarding, and your core value action.

For auth flows, skip the UI login on every test. Use cy.session() to cache the authenticated state across specs. Define a login command once, call it in beforeEach with a session key — Cypress replays the session from cache instead of hitting your login form on every run, cutting suite time dramatically.

For onboarding and core flows, use cy.intercept() to stub non-critical third-party calls (analytics, chat widgets) so flaky network conditions don't kill your signal. Keep assertions behavioral: cy.contains(), cy.location('pathname'), visible element checks — not implementation details like class names.

Structure specs by user journey, not by page. One spec file per critical path: auth.cy.ts, onboarding.cy.ts, core-action.cy.ts. Each should be independently runnable with no shared DOM state leaking between tests. Use cy.clearCookies() and cy.clearLocalStorage() in afterEach if session() isn't in play.

bytes.topic: Mocking — MSW for API mocking, when to mock vs use real dependencies
bytes.title: Cypress + MSW — Service Worker Mocking vs cy.intercept() and When to Pick Each
bytes.body:
Cypress ships `cy.intercept()` for network stubbing, but MSW fills a different niche — particularly in Cypress Component Testing, where the mock lives at the service worker level and your component exercises the same fetch/axios code path as production.

`cy.intercept()` intercepts at the browser network proxy layer. It's ideal for E2E tests: coarse control over full request/response cycles, request aliasing with `cy.wait('@alias')`, and conditional stubbing mid-test. MSW is better for component tests where you want handler logic that mirrors your real API contract and can be reused outside Cypress (Storybook, unit tests).

Set up MSW once in `cypress/support/component.ts` via `setupWorker()`, then override per-test with `worker.use()`. Always call `worker.resetHandlers()` in `afterEach` to prevent handler leakage between tests.

**Mock when:** testing error states (500s, timeouts), CI without a live backend, or any component test where UI logic — not the API — is under test.

**Use real dependencies when:** running E2E critical paths, contract-verifying response shapes, or confirming integration between frontend and live backend. Stale MSW handlers return green tests against a broken API — the most dangerous false positive in frontend testing.

bytes.topic: Async Testing — waitFor, findBy queries, handling promises and fake timers
bytes.title: Cypress Async Testing — cy.intercept, findBy, and Clock Control Done Right
bytes.body:
Cypress commands are already async and chainable — but async testing breaks down when you fight the framework instead of working with it.

Avoid `cy.wait(2000)` for network responses. Use `cy.intercept()` with an alias and `cy.wait('@alias')` instead — this gates on the actual request completing, not an arbitrary timeout.

For DOM assertions, Cypress automatically retries `.should()` and chained queries up to `defaultCommandTimeout` (4s by default). You rarely need `cy.findByText()` from Testing Library unless your team is standardizing on those selectors — native `cy.contains()` and `cy.get()` already retry.

Fake timers shine when testing debounced inputs or polling intervals. Call `cy.clock()` before mounting, then `cy.tick(ms)` to advance time synchronously. Restore with `cy.clock().then(c => c.restore())` or let `afterEach` handle it.

Promise-based utilities returned from `cy.wrap(promise)` integrate cleanly — Cypress queues them into its command chain, so assertions after `cy.wrap()` wait for resolution automatically.

bytes.topic: Snapshot Testing — when it helps vs when it becomes noise
bytes.title: Cypress Snapshot Testing — When It Helps vs When It Becomes Noise
bytes.body:
Cypress doesn't ship snapshot testing natively — you need `cypress-image-snapshot` for pixel-level comparisons or `@cypress/snapshot` for serialized DOM diffs. Visual snapshots via `cy.matchImageSnapshot()` shine for catching unintended layout regressions in stable, design-system components: buttons, cards, nav bars. Run them inside `cy.mount()` component tests in isolation, where the DOM is fully deterministic.

The noise starts when you snapshot pages with dynamic content — timestamps, user avatars, A/B flags, or animations mid-frame. Every CI run produces a different baseline diff, turning your suite into a churn machine. The `failureThreshold` option lets you tolerate minor pixel variance, but abusing it defeats the purpose.

Practical rules: snapshot at the component level, never the full page. Use the `clip` option to scope to a stable region. Gate visual snapshot runs behind a `CYPRESS_SNAPSHOTS` env flag so they don't block every PR — run them nightly or on merges to `main` only. For DOM structure assertions, prefer `cy.get('[data-testid]').should('exist')` over serialized snapshots — DOM diffs break on any minor render change and produce unreadable output.

bytes.topic: Visual Regression — Chromatic with Storybook, screenshot diffing in CI
bytes.title: Cypress Visual Regression — Chromatic for Components, Screenshot Diffing for Flows
bytes.body:
Cypress doesn't ship visual diffing natively — `cy.screenshot()` captures, but `@simonsmith/cypress-image-snapshot` (wrapping pixelmatch) handles the comparison. Register `addMatchImageSnapshotCommand()` in `cypress/support/commands.ts`, then call `cy.matchImageSnapshot('baseline-name')` after your UI has fully settled.

In CI, the first run seeds baselines under `cypress/snapshots/`. Subsequent runs diff against them and fail if pixel delta exceeds `failureThreshold` (default 0.01 — 1%). Gate updates behind `CYPRESS_updateSnapshots=true` so baselines only change on deliberate commits, never on flaky reruns.

Chromatic handles the other half: it runs your Storybook stories headlessly and ships pixel diffs to a cloud dashboard, catching component-level regressions without browser automation. The division of labor matters — Chromatic owns isolated component diffing; Cypress owns interaction-driven page state stories can't replicate. A multi-step wizard, a toast triggered post-API call, a skeleton-to-data transition — these belong to Cypress.

In GitHub Actions, run both in the same job: `chromatic --exit-zero-on-changes` first as a non-blocking review gate, then `cypress run` as your hard failure gate.

bytes.topic: Accessibility Testing — axe-core integration, automated a11y checks in tests
bytes.title: Cypress Accessibility Testing — Automating a11y Checks with axe-core
bytes.body:
Cypress integrates with axe-core via `cypress-axe`, giving you automated WCAG compliance checks directly inside your test suite. After installing `cypress-axe` and `axe-core`, inject the axe runtime with `cy.injectAxe()` after your page loads, then call `cy.checkA11y()` to audit the entire DOM — or scope it to a selector like `cy.checkA11y('#modal')` to isolate a component.

By default, `checkA11y()` fails the test on any axe violation. You can tighten or relax this with the `runOnly` option to target specific WCAG rules, or pass an `includedImpacts` filter to only fail on `critical` and `serious` issues — useful for gradual a11y adoption without blocking CI on minor infractions.

Combine with `cy.get().focus()` or `.tab()` (via `cypress-plugin-tab`) to test keyboard navigation flows. Log violations to the Cypress command log using a custom `violationCallback` so failures surface with rule IDs, impact level, and the offending DOM node — making triage fast without leaving Cypress.

bytes.topic: Test Data Management — factories, fixtures, seeding test state
bytes.title: Cypress Test Data Management — Factories, Fixtures, and Seeding State
bytes.body:
Cypress fixtures (`cy.fixture()`) are great for static snapshots, but they break down when tests need varied or relational data. The fix: factory functions paired with `cy.task()` for server-side seeding.

Define a factory in `cypress/support/factories/user.ts` that returns a typed object with sensible defaults, accepting a partial override. Call `cy.task('seedUser', userFactory({ role: 'admin' }))` to push that record into your test database before the spec runs. Your `cypress.config.ts` registers the task using a DB client — Prisma, Knex, or raw `pg` — so the data is real, not mocked.

For read-only contract data (dropdown options, static config), `cy.fixture('plans.json')` remains the right tool. Use `fixture` as an alias with `.as()` so the data is reachable across hooks: `cy.fixture('plans').as('plans')` then `cy.get('@plans')` in the test body.

Clean up with an `afterEach` task that truncates seeded tables by a test-run ID injected at factory build time. This keeps runs idempotent without full DB resets between every spec.

bytes.topic: CI Integration — parallelizing tests, caching, fail-fast strategies
bytes.title: Cypress CI Integration — Parallelizing Tests, Caching, and Fail-Fast Strategies
bytes.body:
Cypress Cloud's `--parallel` flag splits your spec files across CI machines using historical run data to balance load. Each machine runs `cypress run --record --parallel --ci-build-id $CI_BUILD_ID`, and Cypress Cloud orchestrates spec distribution dynamically — no static sharding needed.

For caching, store `~/.cache/Cypress` (the binary) and `node_modules` separately. The binary rarely changes, so its cache hit rate approaches 100%. On GitHub Actions, key the `node_modules` cache on `hashFiles('**/package-lock.json')` and the Cypress binary cache on the Cypress version from `package.json`.

Fail-fast isn't built into Cypress Cloud natively, but you can approximate it: use `--exit-code-on-failure` in your CI step combined with `--bail` in `cypress.config.ts` (`bail: 1` stops the run after the first failing spec on that machine). For true cross-machine fail-fast, use Cypress Cloud's cancel-on-failure feature, which aborts all parallel runners when any machine reports a failure — configurable under project settings.
bytes.code_snippet:
# .github/workflows/cypress.yml
jobs:
  cypress-run:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        containers: [1, 2, 3]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: ~/.cache/Cypress
          key: cypress-binary-${{ hashFiles('**/package.json') }}
      - uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: node_modules
          key: node-modules-${{ hashFiles('**/package-lock.json') }}
      - run: npx cypress run --record --parallel --ci-build-id ${{ github.run_id }}

bytes.topic: Vitest-specific — vite-native speed, compatible Jest API, in-source testing
bytes.title: Cypress + Vitest — Fast Unit Tests for the Logic Your Cypress Specs Can't Cheaply Cover
bytes.body:
Cypress excels at E2E and component testing, but spinning up a browser to validate a URL builder or a state reducer is wasteful. Vitest fills the unit layer without adding tooling friction — if your project already uses Vite (common with Cypress component testing setups), Vitest shares the same `vite.config.ts` with zero extra config.

The API is Jest-compatible: `describe`, `it`, `expect`, `vi.fn()`, `vi.spyOn()`. If you've written Cypress custom commands or tested pure utility logic before, you're already familiar. Vitest resolves modules through Vite's pipeline, so `import.meta.env` values — the same ones your `cypress.config.ts` reads via `defineConfig` — work identically in tests.

The sharpest Vitest feature is in-source testing: embed test blocks directly inside source files using an `if (import.meta.vitest)` guard. These blocks are tree-shaken out of production builds automatically. Use this for utilities you also reference inside Cypress custom commands — the logic is tested co-located, not buried in a separate `__tests__` folder.

Pair Cypress for integration flows, Vitest for the pure-logic base of your test pyramid.

bytes.topic: Playwright-specific — multi-browser, trace viewer, network interception
bytes.title: Cypress cy.intercept() — Network Mocking, Cross-Browser Runs, and Time-Travel Debugging
bytes.body:
Cypress handles network interception at the browser level via cy.intercept(), giving you full control over request matching, response stubbing, and spy assertions — no proxy setup required.

Use cy.intercept() with an alias to assert on request timing and payload. Unlike Playwright's route(), Cypress waits declaratively: cy.wait('@alias') blocks until the intercepted request resolves, preventing flaky assertions on async UI.

For cross-browser coverage, pass --browser firefox or --browser edge to the Cypress CLI. Component tests and E2E specs run identically across Chromium-family browsers, Firefox, and Electron — the same cy.intercept() hooks apply everywhere without browser-specific conditionals.

Cypress's built-in Test Runner is its trace viewer equivalent. Every command is snapshotted — click the step in the Command Log to time-travel back to that DOM state, inspect the network request body, and see exactly what the app rendered. Combine this with cy.intercept({ log: true }) to surface request/response details inline in the log. No separate trace file to download; debugging is live in the browser.

bytes.title: Cypress cy.intercept + Component Testing — Wire Up Real Network Behavior
bytes.body:
Cypress Component Testing (CT) runs your components in isolation, but real apps talk to APIs. Unlike E2E mode, CT doesn't spin up a full server — so cy.intercept() becomes your only network layer. Mount the component, intercept the request before it fires, and assert on both the stub response and the resulting DOM.

cy.intercept() in CT works identically to E2E: it pattern-matches by method/URL and can return a fixture, a static body, or a routeHandler function that lets you inspect the actual request. Use cy.wait('@alias') to pause the test until the intercepted request completes — this eliminates flaky setTimeout hacks.

A critical CT-specific gotcha: if your component fires a request on mount, declare cy.intercept() before cy.mount(), otherwise the request races past the stub. Also, CT shares no cookies or session state with E2E specs — stub auth headers explicitly inside the routeHandler or via cy.intercept's headers option. Combine with cy.fixture() to keep payloads maintainable across multiple component specs.

bytes.topic: Storybook — component documentation, interaction testing, story-driven development
bytes.title: Cypress + Storybook — Story-Driven Component Testing with composeStories
bytes.body:
Cypress Component Testing integrates directly with Storybook via `composeStories` from `@storybook/react`. Instead of duplicating setup — mocks, decorators, args — across both stories and component tests, you import stories as-is and mount them with `cy.mount()`. The story's args, decorators, and global parameters all compose automatically.

`composeStories` resolves each named export against your global Storybook config, giving you a fully hydrated React component. A `Loading` story that wraps a context provider and pre-sets `isLoading: true` doesn't need to be re-described in your test — mount it directly.

For assertions, Cypress outperforms Storybook's built-in `play` function: use `cy.intercept()` for network-level validation, `cy.get('@spy').should('have.been.calledWith', ...)` for action args wired as stubs, and `cy.wait('@alias')` for async state transitions. No Storybook server is required — `cypress run --component` bundles stories through Cypress's own Vite dev server.

This pattern keeps stories as the single source of truth for component states, eliminates test setup duplication, and gives you real browser assertions that `play` functions simply can't match.

bytes.topic: Code Coverage — what it tells you and what it doesn't, avoiding coverage theater
bytes.title: Cypress Code Coverage — What the Numbers Hide and How to Stop Lying to Yourself
bytes.body:
Cypress can report 90% line coverage while your app has untested failure paths everywhere. Coverage measures execution, not assertions. A `cy.visit('/')` that renders your entire component tree will light up lines green without a single `cy.get()` or `expect()` call.

The real culprits: `cy.visit()` inflates statement coverage by loading every import chain. Stubbed network calls (`cy.intercept`) let error branches go dark. And `cy.get('.btn').click()` may execute 20 lines but never assert what changed.

To instrument properly, use `@cypress/code-coverage` with Babel or Istanbul (`nyc`). It injects counters into your app bundle during `cypress open` or `cypress run`. Coverage is collected per-spec via the `cy.task('coverageReport')` hook, then merged at the end.

What coverage actually tells you: which lines ran. What it doesn't: whether behavior was verified. Track *assertion density* — ratio of `cy.get().should()` calls to `cy.visit()` calls — alongside coverage. A test suite with 85% coverage and 3:1 assertion ratio beats 95% coverage with 0.5:1 every time. Coverage theater fails in production; assertion density catches it in CI.
